title: Fleeing from the Battlefield
subtitle: A Betrayal of Allah, the Believers, and the Covenant of Jihad

verses.arabic: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا زَحْفًا فَلَا تُوَلُّوهُمُ الْأَدْبَارَ
verses.translation: O you who have believed, when you meet those who disbelieve advancing [for battle], do not turn to them your backs [in flight].

verses.arabic: وَمَن يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُ إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفًا لِّقِتَالٍ أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزًا إِلَىٰ فِئَةٍ فَقَدْ بَاءَ بِغَضَبٍ مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَمَأْوَاهُ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ
verses.translation: And whoever turns his back to them on such a day, unless swerving [as a strategy] for battle or joining another company, has certainly returned with anger [upon him] from Allah, and his refuge is Hell — and wretched is the destination.

verses.arabic: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا لَقِيتُمْ فِئَةً فَاثْبُتُوا وَاذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ كَثِيرًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ
verses.translation: O you who have believed, when you encounter a company [from the enemy forces], stand firm and remember Allah much that you may be successful.

brief_explanation: The Quran explicitly condemns fleeing from the battlefield as earning the wrath (ghadab) of Allah and a destination in Hell — one of the clearest divine verdicts on a military act in Islamic scripture. The only exceptions are tactical retreat to regroup or manoeuvre for battle, which are distinguished from cowardly flight. Deserting one's Muslim brothers in the field of combat exposes them to death, weakens the community of believers, and represents a betrayal of the most serious trust — the defence of the Ummah and the cause of Allah. The Prophet (peace be upon him) listed fleeing from the battlefield among the seven destructive (al-mubiqat) sins.
capital_punishment: The Quran prescribes no worldly hadd specifically for fleeing the battlefield. The punishment described is divine — Allah's wrath and Hell in the Hereafter. However, the commander of an Islamic army has wide ta'zir authority to discipline deserters, including imprisonment, demotion, or flogging, according to the scholars. If desertion leads to the death of fellow soldiers or a strategic defeat, the gravity of the crime may warrant severe discretionary punishment determined by the ruler or military court.
